experiences.intentions:
TURKEY: Turkey starts with 3 centers and standard opening tension with Russia and Austria. The key borders are Black Sea, Bulgaria, and Armenia. Russia is the main immediate threat because of Black Sea/Armenia contest; Austria can be ally or later target, but early taking BUL is essential. Best balance is to secure BUL with A CON while contesting BLA with F ANK and keeping A SMY flexible/defensive into ARM to pressure Russia and cover against a southern stab line.
AUSTRIA: Austria starts with 3 centers and is squeezed between Italy, Russia, and Turkey. Key Spring 1901 priorities are securing Serbia and contesting/positioning for Galicia/Rumania while avoiding an early Italian stab on Trieste. Italy is the most immediate tactical threat to Trieste; Russia and Turkey are the main eastern rivals, but Russia can also be a temporary partner against Turkey. Best balance is defensive against Italy while expanding south: move F TRI to Albania, A BUD to Serbia, and A VIE to Galicia to pressure Russia and keep options open.
GERMANY: Germany starts with 3 centers and strong growth chances in Denmark, Holland, and Belgium, but also sits between France, England, and Russia. Immediate priorities are securing Scandinavia/North Sea access and setting up for a western claim while keeping flexibility in the center. England and France are potential rivals over Holland/Belgium and Denmark/North Sea; Russia is a longer-term eastern concern, so I don't want to overcommit east yet. Best opening is standard and flexible: fleet to Denmark, Berlin to Kiel to preserve unit balance and support future builds/coverage, and Munich to Ruhr to pressure/contest Holland or Belgium while guarding the west.
FRANCE: France starts with 3 centers and solid central options. The key 1901 priorities are securing Iberia and contesting/occupying Burgundy to shape relations with Germany while keeping flexibility versus England. Germany is the main immediate land-side threat/competitor; England is a naval rival but not yet directly dangerous in Spring. Italy is a possible concern in Piedmont, but moving to Spain from Marseille covers growth while avoiding needless anti-Italian escalation. Best plan is standard flexible opening: fleet to Mid-Atlantic for Portugal/Spain, army Paris to Burgundy for central leverage, army Marseille to Spain for a guaranteed build path.
ITALY: Italy starts with 3 centers and a flexible central position. Austria is the key immediate neighbor and potential threat/ally; France is less urgent in Spring 1901, while Turkey/Russia matter via the eastern balance. Standard priorities are securing growth while avoiding an early anti-Austrian overcommitment unless coordinated. The best opening is usually to contest/cover the Ionian and position armies for either Lepanto or anti-Austrian options. I’ll attack positionally rather than directly: fleet to Ionian, Rome to Apulia, Venice to hold as a hedge against Austria/Tyrolia.
ENGLAND: England starts with 3 centers and strong naval openings. Immediate strategic priorities are securing Norway in the fall and avoiding an early overcommitment against both France and Germany. France is the main western naval rival; Germany is a possible partner but also contests the North Sea/Denmark region. Russia is a longer-term concern in the north, making northern positioning important. Best opening is defensive-flexible: F EDI to NTH to contest the key sea, F LON to ENG to pressure France and preserve options on Belgium/Brest, and A LVP to YOR to prepare support or convoy-related flexibility next turn. This attacks positionally without exposing home centers.
RUSSIA: Russia starts with 4 centers and strong expansion prospects to RUM plus a northern bounce/claim. Main threats are Turkey in the south and Austria/Germany in the center; England is a longer-term northern concern. Best opening is flexible expansion and anti-Turk posture: move F SEV to BLA to contest Turkey, A WAR to UKR to support pressure on RUM next turn, A MOS to SEV to keep southern reinforcement flowing, and F STP/SC to BOT for northern development toward Sweden while staying noncommittal versus Germany.

experiences.intentions:
FRANCE: France has no extra centers yet but is well placed with A BUR and A SPA plus F MAO. England is the immediate threat with F ENG adjacent to Brest/Picardy and A YOR suggesting pressure on Belgium or convoy possibilities. Germany in RUH/KIE is also a competitor for Belgium and potentially Burgundy, but BUR gives us leverage. Best plan is a balanced defensive-offensive stance: secure Spain by moving to Portugal, cover Brest from English fleet, and take Belgium with support from Burgundy. Key borders are Channel/Brest and Burgundy-Belgium-Ruhr.
RUSSIA: Russia has 4 centers and decent southern posture with A UKR and F SEV adjacent to RUM/BLA, plus F BOT pressuring the north. Biggest immediate threats are Turkey in the south and potentially Germany/England in the north, but Turkey is the most actionable target. Austria in GAL could also contest RUM, so taking/contesting RUM with support is important. Best plan is aggressive south: try for RUM with support, contest BLA, while keeping north flexible by moving BOT to SWE for likely gain and holding MOS as reserve.
ITALY: Italy has 3 centers and three units positioned for flexible eastern expansion: APU and ION can secure Tunis or project toward the Balkans, while VEN guards the Austrian border. Austria is the main immediate threat because A GAL and F ALB indicate pressure near Venice/Trieste, while Turkey and Russia are more distant. With no guaranteed support available to take Greece and Tunis is the safest likely gain. Best is to defend the homeland with VEN holding, move ION to TUN for the center, and bring APU to NAP to keep formation and avoid overcommitting against Austria this turn.
ENGLAND: England has 3 centers and strong naval reach with fleets in ENG and NTH plus A YOR. Immediate priorities are to secure gains while avoiding overextension. France is the main western threat with F MAO and A BUR already advanced; Germany also contests Low Countries/North Sea approaches with F DEN, A KIE, A RUH. Russia is distant for now. Best plan is flexible pressure on Belgium while keeping North Sea and Channel active: convoy YOR to Belgium, support from ENG, and move NTH to Norway for a likely secure build route and reduced exposure to German counterplay.
GERMANY: Germany has 3 centers and currently occupies Denmark, Ruhr, and Kiel after Spring 1901. The immediate priorities are securing one or more builds while avoiding overexposure to France and England. England is a major naval threat with fleets in NTH and ENG plus A YOR, so protecting Denmark/Kiel and contesting Scandinavia matters. France is also dangerous with A BUR adjacent to Ruhr/Kiel and F MAO projecting west; however BUR cannot take German centers alone this turn without support. Russia has F BOT and is the main rival for Sweden, but can be bounced. Best practical plan is to secure Holland with A RUH, support it from A KIE, and send F DEN to Sweden for a likely contested but worthwhile attempt. This is balanced expansion with some defense on the western border.
AUSTRIA: Austria has 3 centers and currently has strong tactical placement: A SER can contest Greece/Rumania, A GAL pressures Russia or covers home centers, and F ALB is key in the Balkans. Main threats are Italy in VEN/APU/ION and the Russo-Turkish presence with A UKR, F SEV, A BUL, A ARM. Since Turkey already occupies BUL and Russia has UKR/SEV, Rumania is hotly contested; meanwhile Greece is still open but Italy can also reach it from APU or support from ION. Best practical outcome is to secure Greece while keeping pressure east. Supporting A SER to GRE with F ALB gives the best chance to gain a center. A GAL to RUM adds pressure for a second gain or at least forces Russia/Turkey to commit there. This is an attack-oriented but balanced set focused on the Balkans, the most important border for Austria now.
TURKEY: Turkey remains on 3 centers with units in ANK, BUL, ARM; no gain yet and Russia is the main immediate threat due to F SEV/A UKR adjacent to Black Sea, Armenia, and Rumania. Austria can be a tactical partner against Russia, while Italy is less urgent this turn. Priority is to pressure Russia and contest expansion while keeping flexibility on southern borders. A BUL - RUM attacks a key border center, supported by Russian options being split; A ARM - SEV directly pressures Sevastopol; F ANK - BLA contests the Black Sea and supports future anti-Russian operations. This is an aggressive anti-Russian stance rather than passive defense.

experiences.intentions:
FRANCE: France has grown to 5 centers and may build 2 units. Current position is strong in the west with gains in BEL and POR, but exposed to England in ENG/NWY and Germany in HOL/KIE/DEN. Italy is less immediate, though western Med matters later. Priorities are to defend the homeland and contest northern waters. Best builds are F BRE to pressure/contain England and A PAR to cover Burgundy/Picardy and react versus Germany or support into Belgium.
TURKEY: Turkey has 4 centers and 3 units, so one build. Position: BUL and ARM give strong pressure on Balkans and Black Sea/Caucasus, while home seas are still open. Main threats are Russia (F SEV, A UKR, A MOS) and Austria (A GAL, F ALB, A GRE); either can contest the Balkans and Black Sea. A fleet build is best for strategic flexibility and defense/offense in the Black Sea and Aegean/Eastern Med. Building in CON keeps ANK and SMY options open later and immediately contests both Black Sea and Aegean lines. Prioritize defense with flexible projection rather than an inland army.
GERMANY: Germany is at 5 centers with 3 units, so we get 2 builds. Position is strong in the west/north with DEN and HOL secured, but pressure is rising: England has ENG/NWY and France has BEL/MAO, so the western front matters most. Russia in BOT is a medium-term concern, but immediate defense and flexibility are key. Best setup is A MUN B to cover Ruhr/Burgundy/Tyrolia and F BER B to contest the Baltic/Denmark/Sweden axis and balance England/Russia. This is a defensive-flexible build rather than overcommitting to one side.
ITALY: Italy has 4 centers and 3 units, so one build is available. Current units A VEN, A NAP, F TUN indicate a western/central posture while Austria has expanded to 4 and Turkey is also at 4; Austria is the immediate land neighbor and potential rival in the east, while France/Germany are less immediate. Building in Rome is the only option. A fleet in Rome strengthens Ionian/Tyrrhenian control and projects against Austria/Turkey while also guarding against France; it is more flexible than an army for the current board.
AUSTRIA: Austria is on 4 centers with units in GAL, ALB, and GRE, so we get one build. The board position suggests immediate pressure from Russia in Galicia/Ukraine and from Turkey via Bulgaria/Armenia, while Italy has units in Venice/Naples/Tunis and could still pressure Trieste or the Balkans. We need a build that helps on the eastern land front first. Building A BUD is strongest: it contests Serbia/Rumania/Galicia lines, supports Vienna, and is flexible against both Russia and Turkey. F TRI would be useful later in the Adriatic/Ionian, but with F ALB already present and A GRE on the peninsula, land defense is more urgent. A TRI is possible, but BUD is the better central defensive-offensive build.
ENGLAND: England has grown to 4 centers by taking Norway and now gets one build. France and Germany are both at 5 and are the main medium-term concerns; France has MAO/BEL/POR and Germany has DEN/KIE/HOL, so both can pressure the North Sea and Low Countries. Russia has only F BOT in the north and is less immediate. With units already in ENG, YOR, and NWY, the key border is the North Sea/home island defense while preserving flexibility to project power. Building F LON is strongest: it secures the seas, contests France/Germany, and keeps convoy/defense options open better than an army or builds in EDI/LVP.

experiences.intentions:
RUSSIA: Russia is on 4 centers with units split between north (F BOT) and south (F SEV, A UKR), while A MOS covers the center. Immediate threats are Turkey/Austria in the south and potential English/German pressure on StP in the north. With no northern support network, the safest active plan is to secure StP, contest Rumania, and avoid overextending. Prioritize defense of home centers and pressure on Turkey rather than a risky anti-German move.
ENGLAND: England has 4 centers and 4 units, with a foothold in Norway but no gain elsewhere yet. France is the clearest immediate threat: French units in BEL, BRE, PAR, MAO pressure the Channel and home waters. Germany is also strong, but France’s proximity to ENG/LON makes the west the urgent front; Russia is a secondary concern in the north with BOT only. Best plan is defensive-containment: secure North Sea, keep Channel pressure, and reposition the army toward coast defense. Capturing NTH is critical because it shields England and opens future leverage against either France or Germany.
FRANCE: France is on 5 centers with forward armies in BEL and POR plus fleets in BRE/MAO; strong west but exposed on the Channel/North frontier and to German armies in HOL/MUN/KIE. England is the clearest immediate threat with F ENG and multiple home units, while Germany is a secondary threat/opportunity around Ruhr/Holland/Burgundy. Best plan is a balanced defense-into-pressure: contest/cover the Channel and Brest, keep Paris/Belgium coordinated against a German push into BUR/RUH, and avoid overextending Portugal. Main borders that matter are ENG/MAO/BRE and BEL/PAR versus HOL/MUN.
GERMANY: Germany has expanded to 5 centers with solid occupation of DEN and HOL, but is exposed on the west and north. The main threats are France in BEL/PAR/BRE/MAO and England in ENG/LON/NWY; Russia is less immediate with only F BOT nearby. France holding BEL is especially dangerous because it pressures HOL/RUH/MUN. Best plan is a defensive-western posture while probing for tactical gain: challenge BEL from HOL with support from KIE, keep MUN ready to cover RUH and central approaches, and reposition fleets to avoid being trapped while contesting Baltic/North Sea lanes. Orders prioritize preventing a French push and maintaining flexibility against England/Russia.
TURKEY: Turkey has 4 centers and 4 units, with BUL gained and a flexible but exposed position. The key fronts are Russia to the north/east and Austria in the Balkans. Russia is the more immediate tactical target because SEV and UKR are adjacent and contest the Black Sea/Rumania axis; Austria is present in GAL/BUD/GRE/ALB but not yet directly threatening Turkish home centers. Best plan is a balanced attack on Russia while keeping defensive integrity: contest Black Sea with support from CON, strike SEV from ARM with possible support from F ANK, and pressure RUM from BUL. Since A BUL cannot be supported to RUM by our own units this turn and ANK supporting ARM->SEV gives the best chance at a gain, we prioritize SEV attack plus Black Sea bid over Balkan adventures.
AUSTRIA: Austria is on 4 centers with units in Gal/Bud/Gre/Alb; position is flexible but exposed on both the Balkan and Italian fronts. Turkey is the clearest immediate threat with A BUL, A ARM, F CON, F ANK and pressure on Greece/Serbia/Rumania; Russia is also nearby with A UKR and F SEV, but can potentially be balanced against Turkey. Italy has A VEN plus fleets in TUN/ROM, so Trieste/Adriatic are vulnerable if we overcommit east. Best course is a balanced anti-Turkish posture while protecting against Italy: take/contest Serbia with A GRE supported by A BUD, send A GAL to RUM with support from BUD to pressure the east, and keep F ALB covering TRI/ION by supporting Greece. This attacks key Balkan borders without leaving the homeland entirely open.
ITALY: Italy has 4 centers and 4 units, with a split posture: two armies in the peninsula/north and two fleets in the south-central Med. Austria is the key immediate neighbor; A GAL/A BUD/F ALB/A GRE suggest Austrian pressure or anti-Turk posture in the Balkans, and VEN is exposed if Austria turns west. Turkey also has active Balkan presence with A BUL plus fleets in CON/ANK, so the Ionian/Tyrrhenian approach matters. France is growing in the west, but there is no immediate direct tactical contest except over western Med lanes. Best is a flexible, moderately defensive setup: secure the central Med by taking ION from TUN, support that with ROM, and reposition armies to improve defense/expansion options. Moving NAP to APU opens the boot for fleet movement and gives army reach toward the east; VEN to TRI is a calculated grab at Austria if uncontested and pressures the Balkans. Borders that matter most are VEN/TRI in the north and ION/TYS in the south.

experiences.intentions: ENGLAND: England is on 4 centers with fleets in NWY and NTH plus A WAL; the immediate issue is preserving naval presence after losing the Channel. France is the main threat, occupying ENG with strong western units, while Germany is a potential counterweight though also strong. In retreat, PIC would be dangerously exposed and likely trapped; LON keeps a fleet on a home center and preserves tactical flexibility, while IRI is useful but less secure and may complicate home defense. Best is to retreat to LON to maintain force count and defend the island.
experiences.tasks: Gain the most supply centres in Diplomacy through strategic orders and alliances.

experiences.intentions:
ENGLAND: England has 4 centers and 4 units, with Norway secured but the home island under immediate pressure from France and Germany. France is the biggest threat: F ENG adjacent to London/Wales, plus A BUR/A PAR/F BRE can support further landings. Germany is also dangerous with A BEL, A KIE, and F HEL positioned for attacks on the east coast and North Sea. Russia is less urgent right now. Priority is defense of the island and trying to retake/secure the North Sea, since that sea controls England’s survival. Best plan is to cut/contest the Channel while supporting F NTH to hold against likely French/German pressure, and keep Norway flexible but not overextended.
AUSTRIA: Austria is on 4 centers with units spread across the Balkans and Galicia; we hold Greece but have lost Trieste to Italy's army in TRI, making the Italian front the most urgent problem. Turkey is also a threat with A BUL plus fleets in CON/ANK and an army in ARM, while Russia has A UKR/F SEV but is not immediately pressing our core if we keep Galicia active. Best plan is primarily defensive against Italy while preserving Balkan flexibility: cut/support against a possible Italian move on Greece, keep Serbia and Budapest coordinated, and use Galicia to pressure/contest Rumania and watch Russia. Retaking Trieste directly is difficult this turn without stronger support, so prioritize holding Greece and maintaining position.
FRANCE: France has 5 centers and 5 units, with strong western gains in BEL and POR but a tense northern posture. Immediate threats are England in NTH/LON/WAL and Germany in BEL/KIE/MUN/HEL; either could contest the Channel/Picardy/Belgium axis. Italy is not an immediate concern, so the key borders are ENG/PIC/BEL/BUR. Best plan is a balanced defense with tactical pressure: keep F ENG active against English coast, support BUR into BEL to dislodge the German army there, hold interior with PAR, keep BRE flexible in support, and walk POR to SPA to secure Iberia efficiently.
ITALY: Italy has 4 centers and 4 units, with a forward army in Trieste and naval presence in Ionian plus fleets around the peninsula. The key issue is the Austria/Turkey frontier: Austria has Galicia/Budapest/Serbia/Albania-facing units and is the immediate land threat, while Turkey is also a medium-term rival in the east. France and Germany are not pressing us now, so the eastern borders matter most. Best plan is flexible defense with opportunism: secure Tunis/home waters, avoid overextension, and probe Austria by taking Albania while keeping Trieste covered against a direct hit on Venice/Trieste lines.
TURKEY: Turkey is on 4 centers with a balanced but exposed position: BUL and CON are vulnerable if Austria or Italy press west, while Russia remains the main immediate tactical target with F SEV and A UKR adjacent. Austria has units in GAL/BUD/SER/ALB and could contest the Balkans, but Italy is also pointed west/ionian, so an Austro-Italian anti-Turk push is not yet fully coordinated. Best plan is a controlled attack on Sevastopol while keeping Black Sea contested and preserving home defense. Borders that matter most are Black Sea/Armenia against Russia and Bulgaria/Constantinople against Austria. Attack Russia, defend key home spaces, and avoid overextending into Greece/Serbia this turn.
RUSSIA: Russia is stuck on 4 centers with pressure on both south and north. The main threats are Turkey in the Black Sea/Rumania theatre and Germany/England in the north via Baltic/Norway, but the immediate tactical opportunity is to contest Rumania while keeping Sevastopol covered. Austria is a possible tactical partner against Turkey, though also a long-term rival near Warsaw. Best is a balanced posture: attack Rumania with support from Ukraine, hold Moscow to avoid overextension, and shift StP to Bot to better guard the north and influence Sweden/Baltic next turn.
GERMANY: Germany sits on 5 centers with units stretched west and north: A BEL and F HEL face the Anglo-French theatre, while A KIE, A MUN, and F BAL guard the homeland and eastern access. Immediate threats are France (A BUR, F ENG, F BRE, A PAR) and England (F NTH, F NWY, A WAL, F LON), especially against Belgium/Holland/Denmark/Kiel. Russia is distant and not yet a direct danger; Austria/Turkey/Italy are irrelevant this turn. Best play is a defensive-flexible posture: preserve Belgium, cover Kiel/Denmark/Holland, and avoid overcommitting into Burgundy while keeping options against England. Supporting A BEL to hold with A MUN protects against likely French pressure from BUR, while F HEL supports A KIE to Denmark to ensure Denmark is secured if contested from NTH. F BAL to Sweden is a low-risk probe for growth and keeps pressure eastward; if it fails, homeland remains covered. A KIE to Denmark improves coastal defense and frees Kiel from immediate naval/convoy pressure.

experiences.intentions:
GERMANY: Germany has 7 centers and 5 units, so gains 2 builds. Position is strong in the west/north with BEL, DEN, SWE and fleets HEL/SWE, but exposed on both fronts. England and France are the main immediate threats: England has F NTH/F NWY/F LON and France has F ENG plus A BUR adjacent to Munich/Belgium. Russia is weaker but F BOT makes the Baltic relevant. Best adjustment is a balanced defense: build F KIE to contest Baltic/Denmark/Heligoland and A BER to reinforce Munich/Kiel/Holland/Belgium against French or English pressure. Borders that matter most are North Sea/Baltic and the western land front.
ITALY: Italy has 5 centers and 4 units, so one build. Position is split between the east (Trieste/Apulia/Ionian) and west-central Med (Tyrrhenian). Austria, Turkey, and Russia are the key eastern concerns; France is a medium-term naval concern in the west, while Germany is growing fast but distant. Given current units, the most urgent border is the eastern front: Trieste is exposed and Austria/Turkey are both nearby. Building A Venice strengthens defense of Trieste and offers flexible pressure into Tyrolia/Trieste/Balkans next year more efficiently than a fleet. Attack posture should remain flexible, but immediate priority is solid land defense and leverage in the east.
TURKEY: We have 5 centers and 4 units, so we get 1 build. Position is strong in the Black Sea/Sevastopol/Bulgaria triangle with Russia reduced to 4 and Austria/Italy nearby. Main threats are Austria in Serbia/Galicia and Italy in Ionian/Trieste/APU; Russia still contests the north but F RUM plus A UKR/A MOS means pressure on SEV/BLA is possible. Best strategic posture is to add an army for Balkan warfare while keeping fleet count at two; an army in CON is flexible toward BUL/SMY/ANK and future pushes on Austria or defense of the homeland. Borders that matter most are Balkans and Black Sea, not Aegean yet.

experiences.intentions:
ENGLAND: England is on 4 centers with a fragile northern position: NWY is our gain but Germany is the biggest threat at 7 centers with strong North Sea adjacency (HEL, DEN, KIE, BEL), while France also pressures us with F ENG and armies on the Channel coast. We should prioritize defense of home waters and retention of Norway rather than overextend. The key borders are NTH/LON/WAL against France-Germany and NWY against possible German or Russian pressure. Best practical line is to force/cover the North Sea with support from LON and NWY, while WAL protects London against a convoy/landing and avoids risky convoy attacks unsupported.
GERMANY: Germany is strong on 7 centers with a broad line from Belgium to Sweden. Main threats are the Anglo-French fleets around NTH/ENG/BRE and the French army in Burgundy adjacent to Munich/Belgium. Russia is weak and not an immediate danger; England and France are the key western threats, though England and France may also be in tension. Best plan is a solid defensive-attack posture: secure Munich against Burgundy, challenge/contain the North Sea from Helgoland and Denmark, and improve fleet positioning with Kie->Hol while keeping Berlin flexible. Belgium can pressure Burgundy while supported by Munich, and Sweden can pressure Norway.
AUSTRIA: Austria is on 4 centers with an exposed but still viable Balkan core: BUD, SER, GRE held, while TRI is occupied by Italy and VIE is temporarily unoccupied. Main threats are Italy in TRI/VEN/APU/ION and the Turkey-Russia presence around BUL/RUM/BLA/UKR. Germany is distant and strong, so survival in the southeast matters most. Best plan is defensive-flexible: cover Vienna by moving GAL to VIE, keep BUD protected, and avoid overcommitting on RUM without enough support. In the south, contest or at least pressure TRI with ALB->TRI supported by SER, since retaking a home center is crucial and Italy may not defend with full strength if A TRI moves or is unsupported.
FRANCE: France has 5 centers and 5 units, holding Iberia plus the core, but is under serious pressure from a very strong Germany (7 centers, armies in BEL/MUN and fleets HEL/KIE/DEN/SWE) and a still-dangerous England with F NTH/F LON and A WAL. The key borders are the Channel/Picardy/Burgundy/Gascony line. Germany is the bigger long-term threat, but England is immediately contesting the Channel coast. Best play is a compact defense that preserves ENG while covering PIC/GAS and preventing opportunistic landings into Brest or Picardy. Support F ENG to hold, keep armies flexible, and cover Gascony against western incursions.
ITALY: Italy has 5 centers and 5 units, with a split posture: two fleets in ION/TYS securing the central Med and three armies in the peninsula/Balkans. The key opportunity is against Austria/Turkey in the Balkans; Austria is weakened and exposed with SER/BUD/GAL while Turkey is also contesting the area from BUL/CON. France is not yet an immediate danger in the west since no French unit is adjacent to Italy, and Germany is far but strong. Best plan is to press Austria from Trieste into Serbia while maintaining home defense and preserving fleet flexibility. VEN supports TRI to SER for the strongest immediate gain; APU shifts to Naples to keep options and cover the boot; fleets hold central positions rather than overextend.
TURKEY: Turkey is on 5 centers with a strong northern setup: SEV, BLA, BUL, CON, ANK. Russia is the immediate threat/opportunity, holding UKR, MOS, and F RUM; Austria and Italy are also close but currently more useful as buffers against each other. The key border is the Black Sea/Rumania/Sevastopol line. Best plan is to press Rumania while covering against counterplay into Sevastopol or Constantinople. Using A SEV to attack RUM with support from F BLA is the most direct gain. A BUL can support that attack. A CON and F ANK hold/cover the homeland and avoid overextension.
RUSSIA: Russia is down to 4 centers and has lost both southern home center Sevastopol and likely northern pressure on StP; position is precarious. Turkey is the immediate existential threat with A SEV and F BLA adjacent to RUM/MOS, while Austria in GAL can also pressure WAR/UKR but may be a secondary threat or possible anti-Turkish partner. Germany is very strong in the north with SWE adjacent to BOT/STP, so I need to cover StP while also contesting Sevastopol. Best balance is defensive-offensive: try to retake SEV with support from UKR and RUM, while BOT moves to STP/SC to shore up the north and MOS supports that move. This guards the key borders: SEV/RUM/UKR in the south and STP in the north.

experiences.intentions:
ITALY: Italy has 5 centres and a dislodged army in Trieste. The immediate concern is preserving unit count if possible. Austria is adjacent and likely hostile given the dislodgement; Turkey is also a medium-term threat in the east, while France/Germany are more distant for now. In retreat phase the key border is the Austrian frontier. Retreating A TRI to TYR keeps the army alive and places it on a tactically valuable border touching Austria, Germany, and Italy, whereas disbanding would concede strength and flexibility.
FRANCE: France has 5 centers and a dislodged army in Burgundy. Germany is the main immediate threat, having grown to 7 centers and occupying Burgundy; England is also hostile with forces nearby, so preserving unit count matters. In retreat phase the key border is the French homeland. Retreating to PAR would block a home center and create congestion; MAR is a valuable southern center and could interfere with defense there. Retreating to RUH keeps the army alive, places it behind German lines, and avoids occupying a French center, though it is risky diplomatically. Best practical choice is to preserve the unit with A BUR R RUH.
experiences.tasks: Gain the most supply centres in Diplomacy through strategic orders and alliances.

experiences.intentions:
ENGLAND: England is on 4 centers with fleets in NWY/NTH/LON and an army in WAL, so the homeland is exposed but we still have naval leverage. France is the immediate tactical threat: F ENG plus A PIC/A RUH create direct danger to London/Yorkshire/Wales. Germany is the larger strategic threat at 7 centers with heavy northern presence, but right now French units are the ones adjacent to our home island. Priority is defense and restoration of naval position by contesting/retaking the English Channel while preventing a landing on London. NTH attacks ENG with support from LON; WAL supports LON to stop French tactical tricks against London; NWY covers NTH against possible German interference.
RUSSIA: Russia is down to 4 centers and has only three units, with the main immediate danger from Turkey in the south and Germany/England in the north. Turkey holds RUM and BLA and can pressure SEV and WAR via BUL/RUM, while Germany is very strong at 7 centers with SWE and DEN, making the north also sensitive. With only A MOS, A UKR, and F STP/SC, the priority is defense and preserving options rather than overextending. In the south, covering SEV and keeping UKR flexible is important; in the north, moving to BOT is the most active defensive posture against German/English northern fleets while staying relevant to STP. Therefore I will support UKR from MOS and advance the fleet to BOT, while UKR holds to guard the southern frontier.
FRANCE: France has 5 centers and 5 units, with strong western naval presence in ENG/BRE and forward armies in PIC/GAS/RUH. Germany is the main threat: it leads on 7 centers and has armies/fleets adjacent through BUR/HOL/HEL/DEN. England is weakened and can be contained; Italy is not an immediate danger. Priority is to pressure Germany while protecting against a possible counter into the French home area. The key borders are Belgium/Holland/Burgundy and the Channel. Best play is to take Belgium with support, keep Ruhr in place to contest Germany, and reposition Brest fleet to Mid-Atlantic for flexibility while Gascony supports the Belgian attack.
GERMANY: Germany is strong on 7 centers with a broad front from Burgundy to Sweden. The main immediate threat is France: A RUH and A PIC/F ENG/F BRE can hit BEL/HOL/BUR/MUN, while England is still dangerous at sea with F NTH/F NWY/F LON but can also be played off against France. Russia is weak and not the priority. This phase should prioritize defense of the western gains while seeking a tactical gain on Ruhr and better northern naval position. I’ll support BUR into RUH, cover Munich with BER, keep HOL/HEL coordinated against North Sea pressure, and use SWE to challenge Norway while DEN falls back to KIE to secure the home line if needed.
TURKEY: Turkey has 5 centers and a solid southern line with armies in BUL/CON/RUM and fleets in ANK/BLA. Russia is weakened to 4 centers and has only A MOS, A UKR, F STP/SC, so the main tactical chance is to pressure SEV/ARM while keeping the homeland secure. Austria is adjacent with A SER/A BUD/A VIE/F TRI; potentially dangerous but currently also a buffer against Italy, so I should avoid overcommitting west unless I can hold BUL/RUM safely. Italy’s ION/TYS and Austria’s TRI make the Balkans a medium-term concern, but immediate borders that matter most are Black Sea, Rumania, and Bulgaria. Best plan is a controlled attack on Sevastopol with support, while repositioning Ankara toward Armenia and keeping Constantinople able to cover Bulgaria if needed.
ITALY: Italy has 5 centers and a stretched but flexible position: fleets in ION/TYS control the central Med, armies in VEN/NAP/TYR can pressure Austria or reposition west. The main immediate border is Austria/Trieste, but Turkey is also growing and any anti-Austrian move that helps Turkey too much is risky. Germany is strongest at 7 and has units near TYR, so exposing the north is dangerous. Best course is a cautious attack posture on Trieste while maintaining central Mediterranean flexibility and keeping Naples from being stranded. TYR and VEN can combine on TRI, while fleets hold useful sea control and NAP shifts to Apulia to support eastern operations or convoy options next turn.
AUSTRIA: Austria is on 4 centers with all four units committed in the Balkans/home line; we have no slack and must avoid losing Trieste or Serbia. Italy is the clearest immediate western threat with A Ven, A Tyr, F Ion, and F Tys pressuring Tri/Adriatic and possibly Greece/Albania. Turkey is also dangerous in the east with A Bul, A Con, F BLA, A RUM-facing pressure; Russia is weaker but A Ukr can complicate Galicia/Rumania. Given the board, best posture is primarily defensive against Italy while contesting the Balkans enough to avoid being rolled. The key borders are Trieste-Venice/Tyrolia and Serbia-Rumania/Bulgaria. I will cover Trieste with support from Vienna, keep fleet flexible in Trieste, and push Serbia to Rumania with Budapest support to challenge Turkish gains without overexposing.

experiences.intentions: FRANCE: France has 5 centers and 3 surviving units after dislodgements, with pressure from both England and especially a strong Germany. This is a retreat phase, so priority is preserving force where possible. F ENG can legally retreat to BRE, IRI, or PIC; BRE is my home center and retreating there would block future builds and leave the fleet trapped, while PIC is vulnerable and congests my army lines. IRI keeps naval presence and preserves options against England/Atlantic defense, so retreat F ENG to IRI. The listed A RUH retreat options appear to be for Germany, not France; as France I should not submit orders for non-French units, so no order for RUH.
experiences.tasks: Gain the most supply centres in Diplomacy through strategic orders and alliances.

experiences.intentions:
ITALY: We have 4 centers but 5 units, so must disband 1. Italy is compressed: fleets in Ionian and Tyrrhenian are important for defending home waters and projecting toward Tunis/Turkey; A Ven protects a home center; A Tyr is flexible against Austria/Germany/France. Austria has surged to 6 and Turkey is still strong, so the eastern front matters most. A Apu is the least essential unit: it is off-center, duplicates homeland defense with nearby fleets, and can be rebuilt later if needed. Disband A APU to preserve naval control and northern flexibility.
FRANCE: France has 6 centers and 4 units, so we get 2 builds. Position is western: A BEL and F IRI pressure England/Germany, while A GAS and F MAO cover Iberia/Atlantic. Main threats are England at sea and Germany on the northern land front; Italy is a secondary concern in the south with TYS/TYR but not immediately adjacent to our home centers. We should strengthen both fronts: build a fleet in BRE to contest ENG/MAO/NAO and support operations versus England, and build an army in PAR to reinforce Belgium/Picardy/Burgundy against Germany while keeping flexibility.
GERMANY: We are at 6 centers but have 7 units, so must disband 1. The main threats are England/France in the northwest and potentially Russia in the east, but Russia is weak at 3 and England/France have nearby fleets. We should preserve core armies (MUN, BER, KIE, RUH) for defense/offense on land and keep key northern fleets. F NTH is our strongest naval asset against both England and France; F HEL also covers home waters and supports Denmark/Holland. F SWE is the most expendable fleet: Russia is weak, and a fleet in Sweden is less critical than NTH/HEL for immediate western defense. Therefore disband F SWE.
experiences.tasks: Gain the most supply centres in Diplomacy through strategic orders and alliances.

experiences.intentions:
GERMANY: Germany has 6 centers and 6 units, with a western-front posture: armies in MUN/BER/KIE/RUH and fleets in HEL/NTH. The main threats are France adjacent via BEL/PAR/GAS and England via ENG/LON/NWY, while Russia is weak and not an immediate danger. Best course is a balanced western defense with a tactical strike on Belgium to pressure France while keeping Munich and the north secure against English/French counterplay.
RUSSIA: Russia is down to 3 centers and in a defensive, precarious position. Immediate threats are Turkey in Sevastopol/Black Sea region and England around StP via NWY, while Germany is nearby but not directly poised to take a center this turn. The key borders are STP, SEV, and WAR/MOS. Best practical stance is defense with a chance to reclaim Sevastopol: move MOS to SEV with support from UKR, while BOT covers STP by moving there directly to block/occupy. This protects the north and contests the south rather than making passive holds.
AUSTRIA: Austria is on 6 centers with a solid Balkan core but only 4 units, so preserving gains while improving position matters. Turkey is the main immediate threat: F BLA plus A BUL/CON and F ARM can pressure RUM and the Balkans. Italy is the other major concern with A VEN, A TYR, F ION, F TYS threatening TRI/VIE and possible Lepanto-style pressure. Russia is weak at 3 centers and can be contained; Germany is distant for now. Best plan is defensive-flexible: secure RUM against a Turkish attack while covering Vienna/Trieste against Italy and keeping options to counter in Galicia or the Balkans next turn.
ITALY: Italy is on 4 centers with a split posture: fleets in Ionian/Tyrrhenian and armies in Venice/Tyrolia. Austria is the immediate land neighbour and has grown to 6 with units in Trieste/Vienna/Budapest/Rumania, so Austrian pressure on Venice/Tyrolia is the main threat. Turkey is also growing, but my current units are better placed to contest Austria first while keeping southern fleets flexible. France in the west is not yet directly threatening Italy, so I should prioritize a solid anti-Austrian stance rather than overextending. Best play is to attack Trieste with support from Venice, while using the fleets to secure Tunis and maintain central Mediterranean flexibility.
TURKEY: We hold a compact 5-center position with strong eastern control: SEV, BLA, ARM, CON, BUL. Russia is weakened to 3 centers and has A UKR/A MOS/F BOT, so the main immediate target is to finish pressure on Russia and secure RUM if possible. Austria at 6 with A RUM/A BUD/A VIE/F TRI is the main regional long-term threat, especially around BUL and the Balkans, but this turn RUM is contested between Austria and Russia and is the key frontier. Best plan is an attack on RUM with support from SEV and BLA while keeping defensive coverage via ARM and CON against tactical slips into ANK/SEV/CON. BUL can pressure SER to test Austrian defenses without compromising the main eastern line.
ENGLAND: We are on 4 centers with a thin but still defensible island position: NWY plus the three home centers, with fleets in ENG/LON/NWY and an army in WAL. The biggest immediate threats are France and Germany. France has IRI, BRE, MAO, BEL, PAR, GAS and is the most direct tactical danger to LON/LVP/WAL; Germany has NTH/HEL and armies on the coast, so a northern stab is also possible. Russia is weak and not the main concern. Given both continental powers are adjacent, best play is defense-first while probing for tactical gain. The critical borders are ENG/LON/WAL/LVP and the North Sea approach. I should try to retake/occupy NTH with support from LON and NWY, while convoying the army from WAL to LVP to reinforce the homeland against French pressure from IRI/GAS/BEL. This sacrifices immediate offense but maximizes chances to recover strategic sea control and secure centers.
FRANCE: France has 6 centers and 6 units, with a strong Atlantic posture but exposed north coast. Immediate threats are England (F ENG, A WAL, F LON) and Germany (RUH/MUN/KIE plus NTH/HEL), while Italy is distant and likely less urgent. Key borders are BEL/PIC/ENG/MAO and the Channel approaches. Best plan is defensive-containment against an Anglo-German stab while preserving options to retake/contest the Channel. Cover Paris/Brest/Gascony and pressure ENG with supports.

experiences.intentions:
GERMANY: Germany is on 6 centers with a strong inland army line but has just lost the North Sea fleet. Main western threats are France and England; preserving naval presence near Denmark/Holand is critical for future defense and counterplay. Among retreat options, HOL is occupied by no German unit currently and keeps the fleet adjacent to HEL/KIE and relevant to both North Sea and Channel theaters. DEN is a home-side center but would block flexibility and is more exposed to follow-up; SKA/NWG are more isolated; EDI/YOR overextend; disband concedes too much. Retreat to HOL best preserves influence on key western borders.
AUSTRIA: Retreat phase only. Austria has 6 centers and a decent Balkan position, but is pressured by both Italy and Turkey: Italy occupies TRI and has fleets in ION/TUN, while Turkey has A SER and naval presence in BLA/ARM. The key border is the Adriatic/Balkans. Preserving the fleet is valuable for contesting the Adriatic and potentially supporting recovery of Trieste; disbanding would leave Austria without naval presence. Between retreat options, ADR is stronger than ALB because it directly contests Italy’s coast and keeps pressure on Trieste/Venice while remaining flexible. Therefore retreat the dislodged fleet to ADR.
experiences.tasks: Gain the most supply centres in Diplomacy through strategic orders and alliances.

experiences.intentions:
AUSTRIA: Austria has 6 centers but only 4 units, so the priority is preserving gains and setting up further pressure. Immediate threats are Turkey in SER/BLA/ARM/CON and Italy in TRI/VEN/ION/TUN; Russia is weak but UKR/SEV can still contest RUM. The key borders are RUM-SER-BUD in the east and TRI/ADR against Italy. Best plan is mostly defensive with tactical pressure: support VIE into BUD to keep central cohesion, cover RUM by moving it to SER, and move ADR to ALB to better contest the Balkans while not exposing home centers.
ITALY: Italy is on 4 centers with a forward but fragile position: A TRI and A VEN are exposed, while F ION/F TUN control central Mediterranean tempo. Austria is the main immediate threat with A BUD, A VIE, A RUM, and F ADR adjacent to Trieste/Ionian lanes; Turkey is also a danger in the Balkans but less directly placed against my home centers this turn. Given Austria’s fleet in ADR and armies nearby, defending VEN/TRI and contesting the seas matters more than speculative attacks. Best plan is to attack/contain Austria tactically by bouncing/pressuring ADR and preserving unit flexibility, while keeping Tunis covered and Ionian central.
ENGLAND: England is stuck on 4 centers with exposed home waters; the key issue is immediate survival against France/Germany pressure. France is the biggest direct threat with F ENG plus armies in BEL/PIC/GAS able to hit London/Yorkshire/Wales, while Germany in HEL/HOL/KIE/RUH is also dangerous but can be balanced if we keep NTH secure. Priority is defense of the island and especially preventing hostile occupation of North Sea. Best practical setup is to contest/hold NTH with support from both adjacent fleets, while army covers Yorkshire from possible convoy/landing routes.
FRANCE: We are France on 6 centers with a strong northern fleet line (BRE/ENG/MAO) and armies in BEL/PIC/GAS. Immediate pressure comes from Germany adjacent in RUH/MUN/HOL/HEL/KIE and from England via NTH/LON/NWY, but England is weaker and Germany is the more dangerous continental rival. Best plan is to attack Germany while keeping the Channel secure and not overexposing Iberia. The key borders are Belgium-Holland-Ruhr and the Channel/North Sea approaches. I’ll try for HOL with support from BEL, cover BEL with ENG support, and shift GAS to Burgundy to increase pressure on MUN/RUH next turn while fleets hold/support.
GERMANY: Germany holds 6 centers with a compact but pressured western position: home centers secure for now, but France is the main immediate threat with A BEL, A PIC, A GAS, F ENG, F BRE, F MAO surrounding the Low Countries and Ruhr/Kiel approaches. England is weaker and can be a useful counterweight; Russia is too small and distant to matter now. Priority is defense of HOL/KIE/MUN line while trying to dislodge or pressure BEL. Best practical move is RUH->BEL with naval support from HOL, while covering KIE and MUN against French incursions using BER and HEL supports and keeping KIE flexible to support MUN.
TURKEY: We have 5 centers and 4 units, with a strong forward posture: Black Sea, Armenia, Constantinople, and Serbia. Russia is down to 3 centers and has A SEV + A UKR, so Sevastopol is the most natural gain. Austria is the bigger medium-term threat with 6 centers and armies in BUD/VIE/RUM plus F ADR, but immediate tactical opportunity is to finish Russia while keeping Serbia from being exposed. Best is a supported attack on SEV from Black Sea with Armenia support; A CON can also head to SEV via convoy route to increase pressure or occupy if bounce patterns favor us, while A SER covers Bulgaria against any Russian stab from RUM/SEV or Austrian opportunism. Key borders are Black Sea/Armenia/Sevastopol in the east and Serbia/Bulgaria versus Austria in the west. This is an attack east with a cautious western cover.
RUSSIA: We are extremely weak at 3 centers and must prioritize survival. Our key position is defending the remaining northern core (MOS/WAR/STP) while looking for any tactical gain in the south. Austria and Turkey are the immediate threats: Austria sits in RUM/BUD/VIE and Turkey has BLA/ARM/CON/SER, giving both pressure on SEV/UKR and the Balkans. Germany/England are more distant, though F STP/SC must stay useful against northern encroachment. Best practical stance is defensive-flexible: cover MOS from a possible Austrian/Turkish push, keep SEV from being easily exploited, and send STP to BOT to stay central for defense of STP/LVN while not getting trapped in FIN. Ordering A UKR - MOS secures a home center with certainty; A SEV H avoids unsupported lunges into RUM/ARM that are unlikely to succeed; F STP/SC - BOT improves northern defense and future options.

experiences.intentions: RUSSIA: Russia is down to 3 centers with only MOS, STP, and WAR, so preserving units matters. This is a retreat phase with only the dislodged A SEV to order. Turkey is the immediate threat, occupying F SEV and F ARM nearby; Austria is also strong in RUM/SER, so the south is hostile. Retreating to UKR keeps the army alive, maintains a presence near MOS/WAR, and is better than disbanding given Russia's low unit count.
experiences.tasks: Gain the most supply centres in Diplomacy through strategic orders and alliances.

experiences.intentions:
ITALY: Italy has 5 centers and 4 units, so gains 1 build. Position is split between the central Med (Tunis/Adriatic) and the Austrian frontier (Venice/Trieste). Austria and Turkey are the main eastern threats; Austria just dropped to 4 while Turkey rose to 6, so the Balkans remain the critical front. France/Germany are distant for now. The key choice is whether to strengthen land pressure in the Balkans or naval flexibility in the Ionian/Tyrrhenian. Since Trieste/Venice already provide two armies on the eastern land front and we currently have only two fleets, adding F NAP best secures the peninsula, contests Ionian/Eastern Med lines, and preserves flexibility against both Turkey and any western surprise. Build, not waive.
TURKEY: We have 6 centers and 4 units, so +2 builds. Positionally we are strong in the southeast with Serbia and Sevastopol, but exposed to Austria/Italy in the Balkans and to residual Russian pressure in the north. Austria is the main immediate land threat with A RUM adjacent to SEV/SER and units in BUD/VIE/ALB; Italy also has ADR/TRI/VEN and could contest the Balkans or Aegean. Russia is weakened to 3 and can be pressured, but fleets matter most now: we need naval presence to defend home centers and project into Black Sea/Aegean/Eastern Med. Best is double fleet build to maximize flexibility against both Austria/Italy while preserving our army count already forward.
experiences.tasks: Gain the most supply centres in Diplomacy through strategic orders and alliances.

experiences.intentions:
AUSTRIA: Austria is down to 4 centers with a compact but exposed core: BUD, VIE, RUM, and F ALB covering Greece/Adriatic. Main threats are Turkey in SER/CON/SEV/ARM and Italy in TRI/ADR/VEN; Russia is weak but UKR can pressure RUM/GAL. Priority is defense and restoration of position, especially preventing Turkish consolidation and watching Italian access to TRI/ADR. Best practical move is to contest Serbia with support from BUD while keeping VIE pointed at Galicia to deter Russia and keeping ALB flexible/defensive in the Adriatic-Greece area.
ITALY: Italy has 5 centers and a compact force around the central Med/Adriatic: VEN, TRI, NAP, TUN with fleets ADR and NAP. Main threats are Turkey in the east (6 centers, fleet presence in eastern seas, army in SER) and residual Austrian armies adjacent to TRI/VEN. France is farther west and not an immediate tactical danger. Best course is balanced pressure eastward while preserving home defense: take/contest Albania as a staging point, move a fleet into Ionian, and keep Venice/Trieste mutually supportive against Austrian/Turkish tactics.
RUSSIA: Russia is down to 3 centers and must prioritize survival: MOS, STP, WAR are all vulnerable. England is the immediate northern threat with F NWY adjacent to STP and can potentially combine with pressure in the north; Germany is also dangerous if we vacate Warsaw/Moscow lines. Turkey is strong in the south with fleets in SEV/ARM and Austria nearby, so UKR must not overextend. Best course is defensive repositioning: secure StP with direct occupation from BOT, keep MOS holding as core anchor, and pull UKR back to WAR to re-cover a home center and avoid being cut off.
GERMANY: We are Germany with 6 centers and 6 units, holding a compact core plus Denmark/Holland/Sweden but under major western pressure. France is the main immediate threat: A BUR, A PIC, A BEL, F ENG, F BRE, F MAO create direct danger to Ruhr, Munich, Holland/Kiel. England is also dangerous at sea with F NTH/F NWY/F LON, but France is the land power on our doorstep, so priority is defending Munich/Kiel/Holland while looking for a tactical gain on Belgium if possible. Russia is weak and not an immediate issue; Austria/Turkey/Italy are distant. Best stance is primarily defensive with a limited counterattack on BEL, using Ruhr and fleet support, while ensuring Munich is not left exposed to Burgundy.
ENGLAND: England is down to 4 centers and under severe western pressure: France has fleets in ENG and BRE plus armies in BEL/PIC/BUR, while Germany also has strong presence in HEL/HOL/KIE/RUH. Immediate priority is homeland defense and preserving NTH/LON/YOR rather than speculative attacks. France is the clearest direct threat because F ENG can hit LON/WAL and continental armies can support landings; Germany is also dangerous but may be checked by France. Best plan is to retake/contest ENG with F NTH supported by F LON, keep A YOR guarding against convoy/landing into English home centers, and keep F NWY flexible while supporting NTH against northern interference.
FRANCE: France has 6 centers and 6 units, with a strong western naval position (ENG/MAO/BRE) and two armies advanced in Belgium/Picardy plus Burgundy. Immediate threats are Germany to the east/northeast and England’s remaining fleets around the North Sea/London. Germany is the more dangerous long-term rival because of adjacent armies in Ruhr/Munich/Kiel/Berlin and fleets in Hel/Hol, but England is vulnerable right now and taking London would be a major gain while also keeping pressure off the Channel. Best balance is an attack on England with a convoy from Belgium to London, supported by ENG, while keeping Burgundy and Picardy in mutually supportive defensive posture and MAO/BRE covering home waters.
TURKEY: We are at 6 centers with a compact eastern position: all Turkish homes plus BUL, SER, SEV. Russia is down to 3 and vulnerable; Austria has only 4 but sits adjacent to SER with armies in BUD/VIE/RUM, so Austria is the more immediate land threat. Italy is nearby via ADR/TRI/ALB but not yet directly on our centers. Best plan is balanced: defend key Turkish cores and Ser/Sev while trying to gain Black Sea and pressure Rumania. Since A CON cannot support F ANK to BLA, the strongest Black Sea attempt is F ANK - BLA supported by F ARM and F SEV. Keep A SER holding/guarding against Austrian action from RUM/BUD, and keep SMY flexible but safe. A CON covers ANK in case of a bounce/dislodgement attempt.

experiences.intentions:
TURKEY: Turkey is on 6 centers and has a strong southern naval line, but this is a retreat phase with only A SER to resolve. Austria remains the main local threat, while Italy is also adjacent via Albania/Adriatic/Ionian. Preserving the extra army matters for Balkan pressure. Retreating to BUL keeps the unit in our sphere and defends a core Balkan center, whereas GRE is more exposed and TRI is risky against Austria/Italy. Therefore prioritize defense and consolidation on the Balkan border.
AUSTRIA: Austria is down to 4 centres with armies in BUD, GAL, and SER, plus a dislodged fleet in ALB. The main immediate threats are Italy and Turkey: Italy has ADR/ION/A ALB and likely caused the dislodgement, while Turkey is strong on 6 centres with BLA/SEV/SMY/ARM and threatens the Balkans. Russia is weak and less immediate. In retreat, the only legal destination is GRE, which is one of our current supply centres. Keeping the fleet preserves unit count and retains a foothold in the southern Balkans/Aegean interface; disbanding would further weaken us. Therefore retreat to GRE is clearly best.
experiences.tasks: Gain the most supply centres in Diplomacy through strategic orders and alliances.

experiences.intentions:
ENGLAND: England is stuck on 4 centers with a very exposed home island: France has F ENG plus A PIC/A BUR and Germany has heavy North Sea-adjacent presence (HEL, HOL, BEL, KIE). Immediate threats are a French/German incursion into London/Yorkshire and loss of the North Sea. Our most critical border is NTH/ENG/LON-YOR. Best plan is defensive containment: retake/contest ENG with support from LON, keep NWY flexible but not abandon northern security, and keep A YOR covering the island rather than speculative convoy attacks.
ITALY: Italy has 5 centers and a forward Balkan posture: A ALB, F ADR, F ION, plus TUN and VEN. Austria is weak at 4 and Turkey is strong at 6; Turkey is the main long-term threat, but Austria is the immediate tactical target because TRI is adjacent and a key gain/denial point. We should pressure Trieste while keeping our fleets coordinated and not exposing Tunis. Best is a supported attack on TRI from ALB with VEN support, while ADR covers/pressures and ION/TUN maintain naval integrity in case of Turkish counterplay.
AUSTRIA: Austria is down to 4 centers and has lost its home centers VIE and TRI, so survival depends on preserving BUD/GRE/RUM and preventing further Turkish/Italian gains. Turkey is the main threat with fleets in BLA/SEV/ARM/SMY and army in BUL; Italy is also dangerous with ADR/ION/ALB/VEN poised against the Balkans. Russia is weak and could be a temporary buffer, not the immediate target. Best plan is defensive with a chance to retake RUM if available: use BUD and GAL to support SER into RUM while F GRE covers BUL/SC to block a Turkish move from BUL and keep Greek flank active.
GERMANY: Germany has 6 centers and 6 units, with an exposed western front: France sits in ENG, PIC, BUR and is the clearest immediate threat to BEL/HOL/KIE/MUN. England in NTH/NWY can be either counterweight or secondary danger, but France is more pressing because of direct army pressure from BUR/PIC plus fleet in ENG. Russia is weak and distant; Austria/Turkey/Italy are not immediate border concerns. Best plan is mainly defensive with tactical counterpressure: secure Munich against BUR, contest Belgium/English Channel approaches, and avoid losing home centers. Support structure around KIE/HOL/BEL matters most.
RUSSIA: We are down to 3 centers and very compressed: only MOS, WAR, STP with units in MOS, WAR, BOT. Immediate threats are Turkey in SEV/ARM/BLA and Austria in GAL/BUD, while Germany also borders us via BER/MUN/KIE but is less immediately adjacent to our remaining centers than the southern pressure. England’s F NWY could contest STP, so BOT should help secure/retake STP. Priority is defense and preserving/strengthening the northern core while creating a chance to recover STP if vacant or lightly contested. WAR should cover MOS by moving to LVN, while MOS backfills WAR. BOT moves to STP/SC to reoccupy or defend the center directly. This keeps two units around STP/WAR/MOS and avoids exposing Moscow to an Austro-Turkish push.
TURKEY: We are on 6 centers with a strong Black Sea/Caucasus position and have already reduced Austria to 4. Russia is weak at 3 centers and vulnerable from Sevastopol/Black Sea, while Austria and Italy remain the main practical threats in the Balkans/Eastern Med. Priority is to defend core Turkish home centers against any tactical stab while pressing for gains in Rum/Gre and keeping flexibility. BLA is the key border: it can support into Rumania and cover Ankara/Constantinople. SMY should head to Aegean to contest Italy and pressure Greece. CON supports BUL to hold against Austrian/Italian pressure from Serbia/Greece/Albania. SEV attacks RUM with BLA support as the cleanest growth chance; ARM holds to avoid self-bounces and preserve coverage.
FRANCE: France has 6 centres and 5 units, so I am due a build later if I keep my centres. Position is concentrated on the north/west front: fleets in MAO/BRE/ENG and armies in PIC/BUR. Germany is the main immediate threat/opportunity: German armies in BEL/KIE/MUN and fleets HEL/HOL put pressure on Belgium/Picardy/Burgundy. England still has LON/NTH/NWY/YOR and can contest the Channel or convoy, but with only 4 centres is more a tactical nuisance than the primary strategic target. Italy and the east are distant. Best plan is a controlled attack on Belgium while keeping Channel/Atlantic security. Taking BEL would raise me to 7 and remove a German forward unit/centre. A PIC can attack BEL with supports from A BUR and F ENG, giving strength 3; this should beat likely German defence unless massively covered. F BRE can support F MAO to hold/cover the west so England cannot easily cut around, and F MAO holds the ocean position. Borders that matter most are BEL/PIC/ENG/MAO.
experiences.tasks: Gain the most supply centres in Diplomacy through strategic orders and alliances.
